title: What is Reinforcement Learning?
concept: Reinforcement learning trains an agent to choose actions that maximize its total reward over time, not just the very next reward it can grab.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In reinforcement learning, what does an agent get after taking an action?

options.text: A reward or a penalty
A pre-written correct answer
A cluster label

exercises.explain: Right! The agent learns from rewards and penalties, not from being told the correct answer up front.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A reinforcement learning agent is told the exact correct action in advance, like a labeled example.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! The agent only discovers how good an action was afterward, through a reward or penalty.

cards.title: Losing a Pawn to Win the Game
cards.body: A chess player sometimes lets an opponent capture a pawn on purpose. It looks like a bad move in the moment — but it sets up a much bigger win a few turns later. Good chess isn't about grabbing every piece available right now.

cards.title: What is reinforcement learning, really?
cards.body: Reinforcement learning trains an agent to pick actions that maximize its total reward over time — the sum of everything it earns across a whole task, not just whatever reward is available on the very next move.

cards.title: Saving allowance instead of spending it today
cards.body: Spending your whole allowance on candy today feels good for five minutes. Saving it toward a bike means waiting longer, but the total payoff is much bigger. An RL agent makes the same kind of trade-off — smaller reward now, or bigger reward later.

cards.title: A delivery robot that skips the shortcut
cards.body: A delivery robot could zip through a narrow alley for a tiny time bonus, but that alley often gets jammed later in the day. A robot maximizing total reward learns to take the slightly longer route that keeps every future delivery on time.

cards.title: It's not about grabbing the nearest reward
cards.body: You might think an RL agent always takes whatever action pays off immediately. That's not the goal — an agent chasing only the next reward can walk right past a much bigger payoff. RL is about the best total outcome, start to finish.

cards.title: Chasing the next reward vs. the total reward
cards.body: Two very different goals for an agent:

left.text:
Grabs whatever pays off
right this instant

right.text:
Weighs the whole task
to earn the most overall

exercises.prompt: Why does the chess player let the opponent capture a pawn on purpose?

options.text: To set up a bigger advantage a few moves later, increasing total reward
Because losing pieces always earns points
Because chess has no reward system

exercises.explain: Exactly! Sacrificing a small reward now can lead to a much larger reward later.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An agent that only ever chases the very next available reward is guaranteed to earn the most reward overall.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Chasing only the next reward can cause an agent to miss a much bigger reward down the line.

exercises.sentence: A reinforcement learning agent chooses actions to maximize its ___ reward, not just its next one.
exercises.explain: Total reward is what the agent is really after — the sum of everything it earns over time.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best shows an agent maximizing reward over time rather than just the next reward?

options.text: A warehouse robot takes a slightly longer path to avoid a low-battery zone, keeping it running all day
A vending machine dispenses a snack the instant a coin is inserted
A calculator adds two numbers together

exercises.explain: Right! Avoiding the low-battery zone costs a little time now but keeps the robot productive far longer — a bigger total payoff.

exercises.prompt: A delivery app agent could take a shortcut that saves 2 minutes now but risks a traffic jam that costs 20 minutes later. What should an agent maximizing total reward do?

options.text: Avoid the shortcut, since the bigger total reward comes from avoiding the likely delay
Always take the shortcut, since any immediate reward is better
Refuse to make a decision
Pick randomly, since rewards don't matter

exercises.explain: Exactly! A 2-minute gain now isn't worth risking a 20-minute loss later — total reward over the whole trip matters most.

exercises.prompt: A trading-game agent could sell a stock right now for a small guaranteed profit, or hold it for a few more turns for a much larger expected profit. It chooses to hold. Why does this fit the goal of reinforcement learning?

options.text: Because the agent is maximizing its total reward over time, not just grabbing the nearest payoff
Because holding stocks always earns more money no matter what
Because the agent was told in advance exactly what would happen
Because reinforcement learning ignores rewards entirely

exercises.explain: Exactly! Choosing the path with the best total payoff, even if it delays a smaller reward, is the core goal of reinforcement learning.

exercises.prompt: Two game-playing agents are compared. Agent A always grabs the nearest coin, even if it walks into a dead end afterward. Agent B sometimes skips a nearby coin to stay on a path that leads to a much bigger score. Which agent is behaving like a well-trained reinforcement learning agent, and why?

options.text: Agent B, because it's optimizing for the best total reward across the whole game, not just the next coin
Agent A, because grabbing every reward immediately is always correct
Neither, because reinforcement learning agents don't take actions
Both equally, because coins are the only thing that matters

exercises.explain: Exactly! Agent B's willingness to skip a small reward for a bigger one later is exactly the total-reward thinking reinforcement learning aims for. Next: let's meet the core vocabulary — agent, environment, and action.
